elements.sent-2411-001: Context-aware methods have achieved remarkable advancements in supervised scene text recognition (STR) by leveraging semantic priors from words.
elements.sent-2411-002: Considering the heterogeneity of text and background in STR, we propose that such contextual priors can be reinterpreted as the relations between textual elements, serving as effective self-supervised labels for representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, textual relations are restricted to the finite size of the dataset due to lexical dependencies, which causes the problem of over-fitting, thus compromising the quality of representation.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this issue, our work introduces a unified framework of Relational Contrastive Learning (RCL) and Masked Image Modeling (MIM) for STR (abbreviated as RCMSTR), which explicitly models the enriched textual relations.
elements.sent-2411-005: For the RCL branch, we first introduce the relational rearrangement module to cultivate new relations on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on this, we further conduct relational contrastive learning to model the intra- and inter-hierarchical relations for frames, sub-words and words.
elements.sent-2411-007: On the other hand, MIM can naturally boost the context information via masking, where we find that the block masking strategy is more effective for STR.
elements.sent-2411-008: For the effective integration of RCL and MIM, we also introduce a novel decoupling design aimed at mitigating the impact of masked images on contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, to enhance the compatibility of MIM with CNNs, we propose the adoption of sparse convolutions and directly sharing the weights with dense convolutions in training.
elements.sent-2411-010: The proposed RCMSTR demonstrates superior performance in various evaluation protocols for different STR-related downstream tasks, outperforming the existing state-of-the-art self-supervised STR techniques.
elements.sent-2411-011: Ablation studies and qualitative experimental results further validate the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-012: The code and pre-trained models will be available at \url{https://github.com/ThunderVVV/RCMSTR .}
elements.sent-2411-013: For scene text recognition (STR), learning useful visual representation directly from unlabeled data is also attractive since a mass of labeled data is usually needed for training to decode the contained text from images~\cite{fang2021read,str2,str3 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, most of the existing contrastive learning and masked image modeling methods are designed for natural images.
elements.sent-2411-015: Directly applying them to scene text images is sub-optimal since the characteristics of scene text images are quite different from natural images.
elements.sent-2411-016: Specifically, scene text images exhibit the following essential characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-017: First, textual foreground and scene background are heterogeneous in scene text images, while text recognition relies primarily on text rather than the background.
elements.sent-2411-018: Second, most text images have only one dominant orientation, $e.g.$, left-to-right reading order for English scripts.
elements.sent-2411-019: Third, besides the whole word, one text image also contains the sequence of characters and phrase structure of multiple granularities along with the reading order.
elements.sent-2411-020: This paper focuses on self-supervised representation learning for STR, seeking to answer the critical question: Can we exploit these text characteristics to learn effective features from scene text images without manual annotation?.
elements.figure-2411-002: \captionsetup{type=figure} \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{mim_intro.drawio.pdf} \caption{We propose RCMSTR, a unified SSL method for text images to fully utilize textual relations. RCMSTR learns richer textual relations in contrastive learning via rearrangement, hierarchy and interaction. Besides, the MIM simultaneously conducts patch and character-level reconstruction to fully learn the local and global relations of text. Finally, RCMSTR is integrated as a unified SSL framework characterized by a decoupled design and effective compatibility with both CNN and ViT.} \label{fig:comparison}
elements.sent-2411-021: Some pioneering works~\cite{aberdam2021sequence,liu2022perceiving,dig have explored how to construct variants of contrastive learning (CL) for text recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-022: SeqCLR~\cite{aberdam2021sequence considers scene text images as a sequence of subwords and thus proposes an instance-mapping function, which makes the atoms of CL to be sequential frames ($i.e.$, the subwords) rather than images ($i.e.$, the whole image).}
elements.sent-2411-023: PerSec~\cite{liu2022perceiving conducts CL on low-level and high-level features, aiming to simultaneously learn the representations from stroke and semantic context.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Masked image modeling (MIM) is another kind of effective SSL approach and some previous works have introduced it to scene text recognition.
elements.sent-2411-025: DiG~\cite{dig directly combines contrastive learning and masked image modeling into a unified model for text recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the gains mainly come from the powerful ViT~\cite{vit , and the masking strategy is the direct transfer of SimMIM~\cite{simmim .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In the same period, MaskOCR~\cite{maskocr proposed to use vertical patches as ViT inputs for MIM, while the vertical patches are too coarse to model detailed stroke relations.}
elements.sent-2411-028: These above SSL methods are mainly transferred from natural images and only partially explore the text characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike them, our idea is rooted in the supervised text recognition in our community, aiming at fully exploring the characteristics of the text.
elements.sent-2411-030: In particular, context-aware methods~\cite{context1,context2,fang2021read achieved great success by incorporating semantic priors from words in a supervised fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-031: We argue that such contextual information can be interpreted as the relations of textual primitives and thus can be utilized in an unsupervised way.
elements.sent-2411-032: This insight motivates the proposed unified framework, named RCMSTR, of contrastive learning and masked image modeling for scene text images.
elements.sent-2411-033: The key challenge here is that textual relations are restricted to the finite size of the dataset, which usually causes the problem of over-fitting due to lexical dependencies~\cite{wan2020vocabulary .}
elements.sent-2411-034: To address this problem, we equip RCMSTR with several novel modules, where the overall comparison of existing methods is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Different from existing CL methods, our text image CL is based on relational CL (RCL)~\cite{zheng2021ressl to model the intra- and inter-hierarchical relations simultaneously with enriched textual relations.}
elements.sent-2411-036: To achieve this, we first create permuted images with diverse relations on the fly by rearranging images.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then, we explicitly model the intra-hierarchical relations of primitives in different granularities, ranging from the words, roots, affixes and characters, to learn semantic information and enhance representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-038: We further propose consistency constraints to explore the inter-hierarchical relations considering their similar attributes in color and stroke.
elements.sent-2411-039: Meanwhile, different from existing MIM methods, we incorporate a block masking strategy into the widely used random masking to learn richer relations.
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, RCMSTR effectively integrates RCL and MIM through a decoupling design, demonstrating compatibility with both ViT and CNN encoders.
elements.sent-2411-041: By decoupling the MIM encoder from CL, the feature mismatch problem of masked and non-masked images is addressed.
elements.sent-2411-042: RCMSTR further utilizes sparse convolution, enhancing its compatibility with CNN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-043: We conduct self-supervised learning on SynthText~\cite{gupta2016synthetic for both CNN and ViT, which are evaluated on downstream tasks, $e.g.$, STR representation quality, supervised fine-tuning, semi-supervised fine-tuning and semantic segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: RCMSTR outperforms all compared SSL algorithms by a large margin on STR representation quality for both CNN and ViT.
elements.sent-2411-045: RCMSTR significantly outperforms SeqMoCo baseline on STR supervised fine-tuning and semi-supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-046: Also, it achieves large performance gains on TextSeg~\cite{textseg for semantic segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Moreover, extensive ablation experiments are conducted to verify our key model components.
elements.sent-2411-048: We first presented Relational Contrastive Learning for Scene Text Recognition (RCLSTR)~\cite{rclstr published in ACMMM-2023.}
elements.sent-2411-049: This paper is a significant extension with the following four improvements:.
elements.sent-2411-050: We present a novel framework featuring new notations, formulas, and diagrams, complemented by comprehensive methodology descriptions and associated learning objectives.
elements.sent-2411-051: We explore the MIM on text images and propose a novel masking strategy with both patch and character-level masking.
elements.sent-2411-052: This innovative MIM design encourages the network to fill in masked characters to promote the learning of global context relations.
elements.sent-2411-053: We design a unified RCLSTR-MIM integrated framework with an effective decoupling design to achieve higher performance.
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, we overcome the ViT-only limitations by leveraging sparse convolution, ensuring thorough compatibility with both CNN and ViT architectures.
elements.sent-2411-055: We evaluate RCMSTR on more STR datasets (12 in total) and more downstream tasks ($e.g.$, text segmentation), showing that RCMSTR surpasses RCLSTR by a large margin, as well as other previous SSL methods.
elements.sent-2411-056: Besides, we conduct our experiments on both CNN and ViT, demonstrating the flexibility of RCMSTR.
elements.sent-2411-057: For natural image self-supervised learning, contrastive learning methods~\cite{npid,cpc,he2020momentum,chen2020simple,swav,byol,simsiam,dino and masked image modeling methods~\cite{mae,beit,simmim,ibot,dinov2 show great success, and they conduct discrimination-based and reconstruction-based pretext task for pre-training, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Contrastive learning methods perform the instance discrimination task to classify different data-augmentation views from the same image into a class.
elements.sent-2411-059: In NPID~\cite{npid , the task of instance discrimination is proposed, and noise contrastive estimation (NCE) is used for contrastive learning, which is further replaced by InfoNCE~\cite{cpc .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: MoCo~\cite{he2020momentum and SimCLR~\cite{chen2020simple improve the quality of learned representations, which propose the momentum encoder and use a single network with a large batch size, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: SwAV~\cite{swav constrains the consistency of cluster allocation of different data-augmentation views.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Some subsequent studies, such as BYOL~\cite{byol , SimSiam~\cite{simsiam , and DINO~\cite{dino , propose asymmetric frameworks that eliminate the need for negative samples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: On the other hand, some works~\cite{zheng2021ressl,wang2022contrastive propose to use KL divergence to constrain relative consistency in the form of similarity distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Masked image modeling methods randomly mask input image patches and perform the reconstruction task.
elements.sent-2411-065: In BEiT~\cite{beit , the visual token reconstruction task is proposed, and they utilize a tokenizer to obtain discrete visual tokens as reconstruction targets.}
elements.sent-2411-066: MAE~\cite{mae and SimMIM~\cite{simmim further replace the reconstruction target from tokens to pixels, overcoming the need for an additional discrete tokenizer.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: More recently, iBOT~\cite{ibot and DINOv2~\cite{dinov2 used the momentum encoder as an online tokenizer.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, these self-supervised methods are designed for natural images, which is quite different from text images.
elements.sent-2411-069: Considering the characteristics of text, we need to specially design the self-supervised method for text images.
elements.sent-2411-070: Some pioneering works~\cite{whatif,aberdam2021sequence,liu2022perceiving,dig,siman,guan2023self explored self-supervised methods in text recognition and have achieved promising results.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We summarize these methods into three main categories.
elements.sent-2411-072: The first approach is based on contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-073: SeqCLR~\cite{aberdam2021sequence maps sequence features of words to instances as atomic elements of contrast learning.}
elements.sent-2411-074: They only consider the text sequence structure.
elements.sent-2411-075: PerSec~\cite{liu2022perceiving proposed to conduct contrastive learning on the low-level stroke and the high-level semantic features of text images corresponding to visual and semantic information.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The second is based on masked image modeling.
elements.sent-2411-077: DiG~\cite{dig proposed a self-supervised framework for text recognition that combines contrastive learning and masked image models(MIM).}
elements.sent-2411-078: Concurrent work~\cite{maskocr also uses MIM for STR.}
elements.sent-2411-079: However, these MIM methods are directly transferred from natural image methods.
elements.sent-2411-080: The third is based on generative learning, and a representative work is SimAN~\cite{siman , which proposes to reconstruct the images from the decoupled content and style information.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In sum, the above methods have not fully explored the characteristics of text images.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our approach takes into account the heterogeneity of texts, the left-to-right structure and the hierarchical structure of the sequence to fully explore the text characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-083: We propose a novel contrastive learning framework to enrich the textual relations via rearrangement, hierarchy and interaction.
elements.sent-2411-084: Recent advancements in text recognition mainly follow the sequence-to-sequence paradigm, known for its flexibility and convenience derived from an end-to-end approach.
elements.sent-2411-085: Text recognition methods based on sequence-to-sequence models can be divided into three categories according to the decoder: CTC-based decoder, attention-based decoder, and transformer-based decoder.
elements.sent-2411-086: The majority of CTC-based text recognition methods~\cite{he2016reading, su2017accurate follow similar pipelines.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The representative CRNN~\cite{shi2016end algorithm is comprised of a CNN feature extractor, an RNN sequence encoder and a CTC decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-088: On the other front, Shi et al.~\cite{shi2018aster introduced a text recognition model that employs an attention decoder alongside a spatial transform network to address the challenges of handling irregular text recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Spatial attention decoders have been further incorporated by Yang et al.~\cite{yang2017learning and Wojna et al.~\cite{wojna2017attention , demonstrating their effectiveness in enhancing text recognition capabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In the recent landscape, spurred by the rapid ascent of Transformers, some text recognition methods~\cite{lee2020recognizing, lu2021master have embraced Transformer decoders in their models.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{main_method.pdf} \caption{Block diagram. Each image in a batch is augmented and processed by the Relational MIM and CL components. In MIM, the image undergoes masking based on a specific strategy to facilitate local and global relational modeling, followed by a prediction head that reconstructs the masked regions. In CL, the image is augmented twice and then fed separately into the online branch (top) and the momentum branch (bottom) of the encoder and projector to create pairs of representation maps. In the module of enriching relations, we randomly permute the image patches and reverse the permutation on their features. Next, for the hierarchical contrastive learning of these representations, we apply three predictors that transform them into frames, subwords and words, respectively. Finally, we apply the relational contrastive loss on the corresponding intra- and inter-hierarchical positive pairs.} \label{fig:rcmstr}
elements.sent-2411-091: We proposed a self-supervised framework for scene text recognition with two branches of relational contrastive learning and relational masked image modeling, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:rcmstr .}
elements.sent-2411-092: First, the input image is augmented to obtain two views (including the first augmented view and the second augmented view), which are input into the online and momentum encoder, respectively, to generate two sets of features and perform relational contrastive loss.
elements.sent-2411-093: On the other hand, the first augmented view is fed into a decoupled masked image modeling branch, with a masking strategy for local and global relational modeling of the image.
elements.sent-2411-094: We use the online encoder and a prediction head to reconstruct the masked images.
elements.sent-2411-095: Finally, the online encoder is updated by back-propagation supervised by the summation of relational contrastive loss and reconstruction loss, while the momentum encoder is a smoothly evolved version of the online encoder.
elements.sent-2411-096: Motivation: Due to the finite size of the dataset, the textual relations are restricted, and the performance of naive relational contrastive learning is limited.
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, we propose to learn the intra- and inter-hierarchical relations with the enriched textual information, building a more complete relational contrastive learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:comparison (a.2), words ($e.g.$, ``justify" and ``notice") can be broken up into subwords of roots and affixes and new words of ``justice" and ``notify'' can be achieved by rearrangement (Sect.~\ref{sec:regularization ).}}
elements.sent-2411-099: For example, the characters (at the lowest level) and subwords (at the middle level) from the same locations (in the same images) share similar attributes in color and stroke, thus showing higher similarity in the feature space (Sect.~\ref{sec:consistency ).}
elements.sent-2411-100: Preliminary: Based on the structure of MoCo~\cite{he2020momentum , an efficient and effective baseline, we propose the relational momentum contrastive learning framework for the CL branch.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The pretext task, instance~\footnote{ Since STR models usually encode a text image as a sequence of features, we will discuss instances at different levels in Sect.~\ref{sec:hierarchical .}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The query view $\mathrm{X _i^q$ and positive view $\mathrm{X _i^p$ are encoded as $\mathbf{q}$ and $\mathbf{p}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: To avoid the need for large batch size, we follow MoCo to maintain a queue of size $K$, and there are $K$ negative features $\{ \mathbf{n _k\}_{k=1 ^K$ from other images.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Then, the contrastive loss of InfoNCE is written as: \begin{equation}0.9\hsize{!}{$\mathcal{L}_{info}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}) = -\log \frac{\exp (\mathbf{q}\cdot \mathbf{p} / \tau_{info})}{\sum_{\mathbf{u} \in \{ \mathbf{n}_k\}_{k=1}^K \cup \{\mathbf{p} \}} \exp(\mathbf{q}\cdot \mathbf{u} / \tau_{info})}\,, $} \label{eq:infoloss}\end{equation} where $\tau_{info}$ is a temperature hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-105: This loss function aims to pull features of positive pairs closer together and push all the other negative examples farther apart.
elements.sent-2411-106: Naive relational contrastive learning~\cite{zheng2021ressl,wang2022contrastive aims at learning not only the relation between query views and positive views but also the relation between query views and negative views.}
elements.sent-2411-107: To achieve this, we calculate the similarity between the positive and the negatives ($i.e.$, $P$) and that between the query and the negatives ($i.e.$, $Q$), and encourage the agreement of two similarity distributions.
elements.sent-2411-108: Formally, we use symmetric Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence as the measure of disagreement, imposing consistency between $P$ and $Q$: \begin{equation} Q_i(q, n) = \frac{\exp(q \cdot n_i / \tau_{kl})}{\sum_{k=1}^K \exp(q \cdot n_k / \tau_{kl})}, P_i(p, n) = \frac{\exp(p \cdot n_i / \tau_{kl})}{\sum_{k=1}^K \exp(p \cdot n_k / \tau_{kl})}, L_{kl}(q, p, n) = \frac{1}{2} D_{KL} (P \Vert Q) + \frac{1}{2} D_{KL} (Q \Vert P),  \label{eq:klloss}\end{equation} where $\tau_{kl}$ is also a temperature hyper-parameter.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} Q_i(q, n) = \frac{\exp(q \cdot n_i / \tau_{kl})}{\sum_{k=1}^K \exp(q \cdot n_k / \tau_{kl})}, P_i(p, n) = \frac{\exp(p \cdot n_i / \tau_{kl})}{\sum_{k=1}^K \exp(p \cdot n_k / \tau_{kl})}, L_{kl}(q, p, n) = \frac{1}{2} D_{KL} (P \Vert Q) + \frac{1}{2} D_{KL} (Q \Vert P),  \label{eq:klloss}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-109: The total relational loss is a weighted average of the InfoNCE loss term and the KL loss term: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{re}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}) = \mathcal{L}_{info}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}) + \alpha \mathcal{L}_{kl}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}), \label{eq:relationloss}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ denotes the coefficient to balance two terms.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}L_{re}(q, p, n) = L_{info}(q, p, n) + \alpha L_{kl}(q, p, n), \label{eq:relationloss}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-110: The first term is the absolute similarity constraint between $q$ and $p$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The second term is the relative similarity constraint, which aims to keep the similarity distribution consistency of $q$ and $p$ with the negatives.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Enriching textual relations via permutation operation
elements.sent-2411-112: Previous works~\cite{wan2020vocabulary,zhang2022context have pointed out that text recognizers are prone to over-dependence on context in supervised learning, while our method advances them further in unsupervised learning.}
elements.sent-2411-113: To alleviate this context-dependent problem, we propose a permutation module to generate new text images.
elements.sent-2411-114: The generated images contain more diversity of context relations, encouraging the encoder not to over-fit finite contexts in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-115: The default process goes like 1) uniformly dividing text images into multiple horizontal patches, 2) randomly shuffling and concatenating patches to generate permuted images, and 3) adding a regularization loss term corresponding to these permuted images after alignment.
elements.sent-2411-116: For the step of image division, we explore different variant operations in ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-117: Specifically, the permutation operation is performed directly on the input images, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:rcmstr .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Firstly, we divide each image horizontally into $N$ patches, where the default $N$ is 2.
elements.sent-2411-119: Next, we take $M$ images as a group to randomly shuffle the $NM$ patches in each group, where the default $M$ is 2.
elements.sent-2411-120: Then, every $N$ patches are concatenated horizontally to achieve new images with the enriched textual information, denoted as $\mathbf{x ^{e}$.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Note that $\mathbf{x ^{e}$ is only fed into the online encoder and projector for features, and such an implementation empirically performs better, likewise the multi-cropping strategy~\cite{swav .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: To align all features of permuted images, we unshuffle them (inverting the random shuffle operation) to put the features back in their original position.
elements.sent-2411-123: We denote the resulting features of regularization as $\mathbf{q ^{e}$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The relational contrastive loss with the enriched images can be written as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{ere}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathcal{L}_{re}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}) + \frac{1}{2}\mathcal{L}_{re}(\mathbf{q}^{e}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{n}),\label{eq:regularization}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{re}$ is from Equation~\ref{eq:relationloss .}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Given that text images are encoded as sequence features, contrastive learning can be implemented across various combinations of elemental features within these sequences.
elements.sent-2411-126: Considering that text words have different granularities in the horizontal direction, we propose a novel hierarchical structure that maps the features to three levels: frame, subword, and word.
elements.sent-2411-127: Thus, we conduct hierarchical relational contrastive learning to learn about relations at each level.
elements.sent-2411-128: To achieve this, we employ three pairs of predictors using average pooling functions with different horizontal window sizes.
elements.sent-2411-129: For frame-level character objects, we use the average pooling function with window size 1 to project encoder outputs to frame-level features.
elements.sent-2411-130: At the subword level, addressing elements like roots and affixes, we average projected features into $T$ subwords ($T=4$ by default) since subwords generally comprise multiple letters.
elements.sent-2411-131: Finally, the word-level representation considers entire words, applying an averaging function to the full feature sequences.
elements.sent-2411-132: At each level, we maintain a separate queue of negative features, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-133: We calculate the relational contrastive losses at each level and sum them up: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{hier} = \sum_{h \in H}\mathcal{L}_{ere}(\mathbf{q}^h, \mathbf{p}^h, \mathbf{n}^h),\end{equation} where $H=\{frame, subword, word\}$.
elements.sent-2411-134: With the proposed hierarchical relational contrastive learning, the model can learn the frame-frame, subword-subword and word-word relations simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-135: Besides the relation within each level, there are semantic relations between features across different hierarchies.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, we propose consistency constraints to learn the relation between neighboring levels.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our default implementation performs frame-subword and subword-word consistency constraints, and we provide the results of other settings in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-138: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:rcmstr , for frame-subword relations, since the frame and subword features from the same spatial locations (in the same images) show higher similarity in the feature space, we treat them as positives and treat features in other locations as negatives.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The subword-word positives and negatives are determined in the same way.
elements.sent-2411-140: For the frame-subword and subword-word relation, we impose the consistency between the similarity distributions by KL loss as the measure of disagreement: \begin{equation} L_{f2s} = L_{kl}(q^{f}, p^{s}, n^{s}), L_{s2w} = L_{kl}(q^{s}, p^{w}, n^{w}), \end{equation} where superscripts of $\{f$, $s$, $w\}$ denote $\{frame$, $subword$, $word\}$, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} L_{f2s} = L_{kl}(q^{f}, p^{s}, n^{s}), L_{s2w} = L_{kl}(q^{s}, p^{w}, n^{w}), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-141: This loss constrains the relation between each feature and its neighboring upper-level feature.
elements.sent-2411-142: Here, we take the positives and negatives from the upper level because we consider that upper-level features contain more semantic information than those at lower-levels.
elements.sent-2411-143: Finally, the total loss of our relational contrastive learning is formulated as: \begin{equation}0.9\hsize{!}{$\mathcal{L}_{RCL} = \underbrace{\sum_{h \in H}\mathcal{L}_{ere}(\mathbf{q}^h, \mathbf{p}^h, \mathbf{n}^h)}_{\text{Intra-hierarchical relations}} + \underbrace{\vphantom{\sum_{h \in H}L_{ere}(\mathbf{q}^h, \mathbf{p}^h, \mathbf{n}^h)}\mathcal{L}_{f2s} + \mathcal{L}_{s2w}}_{\text{Inter-hierarchical relations}}$}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}0.9\hsize{!}{$L_{RCL} = \underbrace{\sum_{h \in H}L_{ere}(q^h, p^h, n^h)}_{Intra-hierarchical relations} + \underbrace{\vphantom{\sum_{h \in H}L_{ere}(q^h, p^h, n^h)}L_{f2s} + L_{s2w}}_{Inter-hierarchical relations}$}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-144: Motivation: Contrastive learning enables feature representation with better discrimination but fails to capture the structure and texture information in spatial dimensions~\cite{CLMIM .}
elements.sent-2411-145: However, the low-level strokes, $e.g.$, font, color and style of texts, are also necessary for STR~\cite{liu2022perceiving .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Therefore, we propose the relational MIM framework for text recognition.
elements.sent-2411-147: In Sect.~\ref{sec:mim1 , we propose a relational masking strategy to fully model the relations based on local and global reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-148: In Sect.~\ref{sec:mim2 , we design a decoupling framework to effectively integrate the capabilities of relation CL and MIM.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Meanwhile, we propose a carefully designed framework that integrates powerful MIM capabilities for CNN architectures in Sect.~\ref{sec:mim3 .}
elements.sent-2411-150: Scene text images typically exhibit two levels of structure: the local stroke structure and the global sequential characters structure.
elements.sent-2411-151: Previous text MIM methods~\cite{dig did not consider both levels simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Thereby, the relations cannot be fully captured.
elements.sent-2411-153: To overcome this limitation, we propose to simultaneously conduct patch-level and character-level reconstruction to comprehensively model the local and global relationships in text.
elements.sent-2411-154: For the modeling of local relations of text images, a random masking strategy of image patch alignment is adopted, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mim_local .}
elements.sent-2411-155: Given that the image patch is the fundamental processing unit for ViT, applying masks at the patch level is both straightforward and effective.
elements.sent-2411-156: Each image patch is either entirely visible or entirely masked.
elements.sent-2411-157: For the modeling of the global relations of text images, we propose the block-masking strategy in the horizontal direction, as depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:mim_global , where a fixed pixel width is used to define a horizontal block.}
elements.sent-2411-158: We randomly select blocks in the horizontal direction of the image to mask, setting the default number of masked blocks to 1.
elements.sent-2411-159: In the ablation experiment, we study the performance of different numbers of block-masking.
elements.sent-2411-160: Reconstruction loss: We employ the normalized RGB values of the original pixels as the targets for reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-161: Each pixel value in the original image is initially normalized to fall within the $[0, 1]$ range, serving as the label for reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-162: MSE loss, $i.e.$, $L_2$ loss, is used for constraint: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{MIM} = \frac{1}{3N} \sum_{i \in N}\sum_{j \in \{R,G,B\}}{(x_{ij} - y_{ij})^2},\end{equation} where $x_{ij}$ and $y_{ij}$ are the predicted pixel value and the original pixel value, respectively, and $N$ is the number of masked pixels.
elements.sent-2411-163: Contrastive learning and MIM are utilized for self-supervised learning on text images for different purposes.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, effectively integrating the capabilities of these two methodologies poses a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-165: We found that directly coupling both methods~\footnote{The coupling design denotes feeding masked images into the CL online encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-166: The resulting feature would be utilized for both MIM reconstruction and serving as positive features for CL.
elements.sent-2411-167: The diagram of this coupling design can be referenced in the DiG~\cite{dig paper.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Similar instability issues have also been reported in previous research~\cite{ibot , attributed to the influence of masked and non-masked positive image pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-169: We hypothesize that it may arise from the distribution mismatch of masked and non-masked images in contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-170: To alleviate this instability problem, we propose a decoupling design that involves separately feeding non-masked and masked images into contrastive learning and MIM, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-171: The decoupling design ensures that the contrastive learning part does not receive masked images as input, thereby mitigating instability issues.
elements.sent-2411-172: The meaning of decoupling is that masked images are only used for reconstruction, while contrastive learning only utilizes unmasked images.
elements.sent-2411-173: Specifically, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:rcmstr , the input image undergoes data augmentation, resulting in three augmented views.}
elements.sent-2411-174: The first augmented view undergoes image masking and is then individually fed into the MIM component.
elements.sent-2411-175: The other two augmented views, without image masking, are utilized as positive pairs and fed into two branches of contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-176: At the same time, weight sharing is employed between the MIM encoder and the online encoder of contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-177: Consequently, our framework reduces the interference between the two components and enables the encoders to derive benefits from the learning gains of both.
elements.sent-2411-178: We provide pseudocode in supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-179: The final loss is the weighted summation of the relational contrastive loss $L_{RCL}$ and masked image modeling loss $L_{MIM}$.
elements.sent-2411-180: It is denoted as \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{MIM} + \beta \mathcal{L}_{RCL},\end{equation} where $L_{RCL}$ and $L_{MIM}$ represent loss functions for RCL and MIM, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-181: Previous text recognition CL-MIM integrated methods~\cite{dig were exclusively designed for ViT, but they are unsuitable for CNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Despite the performance advantages of ViT-based text recognition methods over CNN, many text recognition applications still employ CNN-based methods due to their computational and deployment efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-183: Therefore, we aspire to incorporate the capability of MIM into the architectures of CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-184: However, integrating MIM into CNN methods for text recognition encounters two main challenges.
elements.sent-2411-185: The primary challenge is how to adapt CNN to ensure the effectiveness of MIM, as a study by L.
elements.sent-2411-186: Jing et al.~\cite{maskedsiamese suggests that directly masking images is ineffective.}
elements.sent-2411-187: The second challenge lies in effectively integrating contrastive learning and MIM in a unified CNN framework.
elements.sent-2411-188: Regarding the solution to these challenges, our key idea is to replace all standard convolutions with sparse convolutions, which is inspired by ConvNextv2~\cite{convnextv2 .}
elements.sent-2411-189: ConvNextv2 discovered that MIM pre-training with sparse convolutions is effective for fine-tuning with standard convolution without requiring additional handling.
elements.sent-2411-190: Inspired by this idea, we propose to use sparse convolution to realize MIM pre-training --- the masked inputs are treated as sparse patches processed by the sparse convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-191: However, the contrastive learning branch originally used standard convolutions, which is incompatible.
elements.sent-2411-192: We found that sparse convolution is actually equivalent to standard convolution for the original, unmasked inputs.
elements.sent-2411-193: So, we also use sparse convolutions in the CL branch.
elements.sent-2411-194: Therefore, both branches can be implemented in a unified way.
elements.sent-2411-195: This approach enables the adaptability of MIM on CNNs and effectively combines the capabilities of both.
elements.sent-2411-196: We conduct our experiments on public datasets of scene text recognition.
elements.sent-2411-197: For our self-supervised framework pre-training, we follow SeqCLR~\cite{aberdam2021sequence to use the synthetic dataset SynthText~\cite{gupta2016synthetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: For evaluation, we use twelve real-scene text datasets, including IIIT5K-Words (IIIT5K)~\cite{IIIT5K , ICDAR 2003 (IC03)~\cite{ic03 , ICDAR 2013 (IC13)~\cite{ic13 , Street View Text (SVT)~\cite{svt , ICDAR 2015 (IC15)~\cite{ic15 , Street View Text Perspective (SVTP)~\cite{svtp , CUTE80~\cite{cute , COCOText-Validation (COCO)~\cite{cocotext , CTW dataset~\cite{ctw , Total-Text dataset (TT)~\cite{tttext , weakly occluded scene text (WOST)~\cite{ost and heavily occluded scene text (HOST)~\cite{ost .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: They covered regular, perspective and occluded text images.. Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-200: To evaluate the STR performance, we adopt the metrics of word-level accuracy (Acc), which calculates the number of correctly predicted words divided by the total number of words.
elements.sent-2411-201: For the text segmentation task, we assess the performance using the average intersection over union (IoU).. RCL Network Configurations.
elements.sent-2411-202: For the RCL branch, we get two data augmentation views and feed them into two separate branches of encoder and head for contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-203: Data Augmentation: For the data augmentation, we follow SeqCLR~\cite{aberdam2021sequence , and our augmentation consists of a random subset of linear contrast, blur, sharpen, crop, perspective transform and piecewise affine operations.}
elements.sent-2411-204: We further apply the masking strategy to the first view for MIM, which will be explained in the RMIM network configuration.
elements.sent-2411-205: Encoder: When using CNN architectures, we take blocks of transformation (TPS, Thin Plate Spine)~\cite{shi2016robust and CNN feature extraction as the encoder, replacing all standard convolutions with sparse convolutions.}
elements.sent-2411-206: When using the ViT encoder, We use a vanilla ViT~\cite{vit as the feature encoder with a patch size of $4 \times 4$.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Projector: When using CNN architectures, we follow the SeqCLR~\cite{aberdam2021sequence setting to use a two-layer Bidirectional-LSTM (BiLSTM) with 256 hidden units as the projector.}
elements.sent-2411-208: When using ViT architectures, we follow DiG~\cite{dig , and the projector is a 3-layer MLP.}
elements.sent-2411-209: In MLP, each layer has a fully connected layer and a normalized layer.
elements.sent-2411-210: Predictor: We set three predictors for the frame, subword, and word levels, each with its own window size.
elements.sent-2411-211: For each level, we use an adaptive averaging layer with the corresponding window size, followed by a final FC layer.
elements.sent-2411-212: When using ViT architectures, we follow DiG~\cite{dig , and the last layer is a 3-layer MLP.}
elements.sent-2411-214: For the RMIM branch, we feed the masked images into the feature encoder and use an MLP layer as the prediction head.
elements.sent-2411-215: Masking: When using CNN architectures, we follow the CNN masking method in ConvNextV2~\cite{convnextv2 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: As the convolutional model has a hierarchical design, the mask is generated and upsampled for all stages.
elements.sent-2411-217: When using ViT architectures, our masking strategy consists of small random patches and horizontal blocks, which are superimposed to obtain the final image mask.
elements.sent-2411-218: Encoder: When using CNN architectures, we use the same CNN network as in the RCL but replace all standard convolutions with sparse convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-219: When using ViT architectures, we use the same ViT~\cite{vit as in the RCL.}
elements.sent-2411-220: We follow the typical self-supervised method SimMIM~\cite{simmim to replace each masked image block with a shared learnable mask embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Prediction head: When using CNN architectures, we follow ConvNextV2~\cite{convnextv2 and use a ConvNext block as the prediction head.}
elements.sent-2411-222: It consists of three convolutions with a norm and activation layer.
elements.sent-2411-223: When using ViT architectures, we follow DiG~\cite{dig , and use the MLP layer as the prediction head.}

elements.sent-2411-286: TextSeg~\cite{textseg is a finely annotated dataset designed for text segmentation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Note that TextSeg is proposed for text segmentation on the whole image, while our task is performed on text patches.
elements.sent-2411-288: Therefore, we first obtain text patches using the bounding box annotations, ensuring that only one text instance is contained within each patch.
elements.sent-2411-289: During both training and evaluation phases, the text patches are resized to 32 × 128.

elements.sent-2411-326: Our baseline method is a common randomly patch-masked MIM, which is denoted as the baseline method SimMIM~\cite{simmim .}
elements.sent-2411-327: Based on the random patch masking MIM, we add the horizontal block masking strategy.
elements.sent-2411-328: The patch masking masks local elements like strokes, while the block masking masks global elements like characters.
elements.sent-2411-329: Experimental results of transformer-based decoders are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:fig_ratio .}
elements.sent-2411-330: For various masked ratios, our patch and block joint masking always achieves higher performance than patch-only masking.
elements.sent-2411-331: For our default masked ratio of 0.7, the accuracy of transformer-based decoder is improved by an average of 1.54\% due to the use of both random patch masking and horizontal block masking strategies.
elements.sent-2411-332: This indicates that the local and global relational modeling of text images in MIM can achieve better representation quality.
elements.sent-2411-334: We applied different numbers of horizontal masked blocks for the ablation study.
elements.sent-2411-335: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:fig_blocknum , different amounts of horizontal blocks play the role of global relational modeling, so the performance is always better than normal random patch masking.}
elements.sent-2411-336: The masking of 1 block achieves the highest performance improvement, and increasing the number of block masks has no further performance gain.
elements.sent-2411-337: This suggests that excessive masked blocks are sub-optimal, as they render the reconstruction task overly challenging and fail to further enhance self-supervised performance.
elements.sent-2411-338: This work proposes a novel framework, Relational Contrastive Learning and Masked Image Modeling for Scene Text Recognition (RCMSTR).
elements.sent-2411-339: To take advantage of contextual priors in STR, we argue that contextual information can be interpreted as the relations of textual primitives and utilized in an unsupervised way.
elements.sent-2411-340: For relational contrastive learning, we propose RCLSTR with three modules to fully utilize the textual relations.
elements.sent-2411-341: With the permutation module, the textual relations are enriched on the fly, while the intra- and inter-hierarchy relational contrastive learning facilitates the capture of multi-granularity representations and interactions across different hierarchical levels, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-342: Furthermore, we construct a unified RCMSTR framework for RCLSTR and relational MIM, and this framework further has three key contributions.
elements.sent-2411-343: First, we propose a joint reconstruction of masked patches and characters to fully learn about local and global relations.
elements.sent-2411-344: Second, our decoupled weight-sharing design effectively integrates the capabilities of both elements.
elements.sent-2411-345: Besides, our carefully designed framework integrates powerful MIM capabilities for CNN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-346: Extensive experiments verify the superiority of our RCMSTR method.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
